title: Sedation Emergency – Oversedation & Respiratory Depression
description: A routine upper endoscopy patient develops progressive oversedation after cumulative propofol and opioid dosing. Practise recognising and grading sedation depth, managing airway and ventilation, and using reversal agents appropriately.
learningObjectives: Recognise the signs of oversedation on the MOASS scale
Identify hypercapnia on capnography before SpO2 drops
Perform stepwise airway manoeuvres (jaw thrust, chin lift, bag-mask ventilation)
Administer flumazenil and naloxone at correct doses for reversal
Understand the limitations and risks of reversal agents

states.prompt: An upper endoscopy is underway. The patient received midazolam 2 mg + fentanyl 75 mcg + propofol 80 mg over 10 minutes. EtCO2 has risen from 38 to 52 mmHg. SpO2 is 96% (still acceptable). Respiratory rate dropped from 16 to 8/min. MOASS is 2 (responds only to vigorous stimulation). What is the EARLIEST indicator that oversedation is developing?

options.label: Rising EtCO2 (capnography change before SpO2 drop)
MOASS level change is the earliest indicator

explanations: Correct! Capnography (EtCO2 rise) precedes SpO2 desaturation by 60–90 seconds in patients on supplemental oxygen. It is the earliest warning of hypoventilation.
SpO2 drop is a late sign — especially on supplemental oxygen. By the time SpO2 falls, significant hypoventilation has occurred.
MOASS reflects depth of sedation, not ventilation. A patient can be deeply sedated (MOASS 1) but still ventilating.
Respiratory rate < 8 is concerning but lags behind capnography changes as an early warning.

checklistItems.description: Identifies rising EtCO2 as the earliest oversedation warning

states.prompt: EtCO2 is now 62 mmHg. SpO2 dropped to 88%. RR 5/min. MOASS 1 (unresponsive). The patient's jaw is falling back, obstructing the airway. What is your FIRST intervention?

options.label: Jaw thrust and chin lift to open the airway
Immediately call for emergent RSI intubation
Sternal rub to stimulate the patient

explanations: Correct! Airway positioning (jaw thrust, chin lift) is always the first-line response to obstruction. This simple manoeuvre often resolves hypoxaemia without drugs or intubation.
Naloxone reverses only the opioid component — but midazolam and propofol also contributed. Airway manoeuvres take priority over pharmacological reversal.
Intubation is a last resort. Simple airway manoeuvres and ventilation should be attempted first.
Sternal rub may temporarily increase arousal but does not address the mechanical airway obstruction.

checklistItems.description: Correctly performs airway manoeuvre as first intervention

states.prompt: Airway now patent with jaw thrust. SpO2 recovering to 92%. Bag-mask ventilation in progress. Despite stimulation, the patient remains MOASS 1. Given the drug history (midazolam 2 mg + fentanyl 75 mcg + propofol 80 mg), which reversal strategy is appropriate?

options.label: Flumazenil 0.2 mg IV (for midazolam) AND naloxone 0.04–0.1 mg IV titrated (for fentanyl)
Naloxone 0.4 mg IV only – this is likely pure opioid toxicity
Flumazenil 1 mg IV (full reversal dose) as a single bolus
Avoid reversal agents – bag-mask ventilation is sufficient

explanations: Correct! Both benzodiazepine and opioid reversal are indicated given the polypharmacy. Naloxone should be titrated (low dose) to avoid precipitating acute opioid withdrawal or sympathetic surge.
Naloxone alone reverses only fentanyl. Midazolam 2 mg was also given – flumazenil is needed to address the benzodiazepine component.
Full-dose flumazenil (1 mg) can precipitate benzodiazepine withdrawal seizures. The recommended approach is 0.2 mg IV increments titrated to arousal.
Bag-mask is essential but reversal agents are indicated to facilitate airway recovery and reduce the need for intubation.

checklistItems.description: Administers both flumazenil and titrated naloxone

states.prompt: The patient awoke after reversal. SpO2 recovered to 97%. 45 minutes later, the patient becomes drowsy again (MOASS 2–3), EtCO2 rising to 48 mmHg. What phenomenon is occurring and how do you manage it?

options.label: Re-narcotisation – naloxone has a shorter half-life than fentanyl; titrate repeat naloxone doses and extend monitoring
New adverse event – restart full resuscitation protocol
Flumazenil effect wore off – give 1 mg flumazenil IV push
Propofol redistribution – no treatment needed, monitor only

explanations: Correct! Re-narcotisation occurs because naloxone (half-life 30–90 min) is shorter than fentanyl (2–4 hrs). Repeated low-dose naloxone or infusion and extended monitoring are required.
Re-narcotisation is expected and predictable after opioid reversal. Full resuscitation is not indicated while the patient is maintaining airway reflexes.
Flumazenil's duration (1–2 hrs) is also shorter than midazolam, but the primary cause here is opioid re-narcotisation from fentanyl.
Propofol context sensitivity does play a role but fentanyl re-narcotisation is the primary concern; active management is required.

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• EtCO2 rise precedes SpO2 drop – use capnography
• Airway manoeuvres FIRST before pharmacological reversal
• Titrate naloxone (0.04–0.1 mg) to avoid withdrawal/sympathetic surge
• Re-narcotisation risk: naloxone t½ < fentanyl t½ – extend monitoring
